ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies a known technique (magnitude-based weight pruning) to a new application domain (deep LSTM attention-based NMT) and claims to be the first to extend this pruning to recurrent LSTM NMT models, which is a useful but incremental novelty. The experimental findings (40% pruning with little loss, 80% with retraining, and class-blind pruning outperforming other schemes) provide empirical insights specific to NMT but do not introduce fundamentally new algorithms. The authors also admit they did not use iterative prune-and-retrain pipelines (e.g., Han et al. 2015b) or compare to other pruning/saliency methods, which limits methodological novelty and positions the work as an application/empirical study rather than a substantive new technique. | B(3): The paper applies an existing magnitude-based weight pruning approach to recurrent LSTM-based NMT and reports empirical findings (e.g., 40% pruning with little loss, 80% pruning recoverable with retraining), claiming to be the first to extend this technique to LSTM NMT. This is a useful empirical extension and provides novel comparative results (class-blind vs class-uniform/class-distribution) but does not introduce a fundamentally new pruning method or theoretical perspective—magnitude pruning and iterative prune/retrain ideas are established (e.g., Han et al. 2015b), and the authors explicitly did not implement the iterative pipeline. Overall, the contribution is an incremental yet informative application and empirical study rather than a highly novel methodological advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a known technique (magnitude-based weight pruning) to a new application domain (deep LSTM attention-based NMT) and claims to be the first to extend this pruning to recurrent LSTM NMT models, which is a useful but incremental novelty. The experimental findings (40% pruning with little loss, 80% with retraining, and class-blind pruning outperforming other schemes) provide empirical insights specific to NMT but do not introduce fundamentally new algorithms. The authors also admit they did not use iterative prune-and-retrain pipelines (e.g., Han et al. 2015b) or compare to other pruning/saliency methods, which limits methodological novelty and positions the work as an application/empirical study rather than a substantive new technique.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies an existing magnitude-based weight pruning approach to recurrent LSTM-based NMT and reports empirical findings (e.g., 40% pruning with little loss, 80% pruning recoverable with retraining), claiming to be the first to extend this technique to LSTM NMT. This is a useful empirical extension and provides novel comparative results (class-blind vs class-uniform/class-distribution) but does not introduce a fundamentally new pruning method or theoretical perspective—magnitude pruning and iterative prune/retrain ideas are established (e.g., Han et al. 2015b), and the authors explicitly did not implement the iterative pipeline. Overall, the contribution is an incremental yet informative application and empirical study rather than a highly novel methodological advance.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes — compressing NMT models via pruning is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope, as the paper targets large LSTM attention-based NMT on WMT'14 English–German. The methods and evaluations match community standards (magnitude-based pruning variants, comparisons to an unpruned Luong et al. baseline, BLEU and perplexity on newstest2014). The work is positioned appropriately — extending pruning to recurrent LSTM NMT, comparing pruning schemes, and acknowledging connections to prior compression work and limitations, making it relevant to the venue's audience. | B(5): The paper addresses neural model compression for NMT, a topic squarely within top NLP/ML venues, and evaluates on the standard WMT'14 English–German testset using BLEU and perplexity. It targets the right audience by extending magnitude-based pruning to recurrent LSTM-based NMT (claimed first for this architecture) and comparing multiple pruning schemes against a recognized attention-based NMT baseline. The methods and evaluation metrics are those expected at these venues, and the paper situates itself within related work while noting clear limitations (e.g., no sparse-speedup implementation, no iterative prune-and-retrain), which are appropriate to discuss rather than excluding it from scope.

reviewer_a.justification: Yes — compressing NMT models via pruning is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope, as the paper targets large LSTM attention-based NMT on WMT'14 English–German. The methods and evaluations match community standards (magnitude-based pruning variants, comparisons to an unpruned Luong et al. baseline, BLEU and perplexity on newstest2014). The work is positioned appropriately — extending pruning to recurrent LSTM NMT, comparing pruning schemes, and acknowledging connections to prior compression work and limitations, making it relevant to the venue's audience.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses neural model compression for NMT, a topic squarely within top NLP/ML venues, and evaluates on the standard WMT'14 English–German testset using BLEU and perplexity. It targets the right audience by extending magnitude-based pruning to recurrent LSTM-based NMT (claimed first for this architecture) and comparing multiple pruning schemes against a recognized attention-based NMT baseline. The methods and evaluation metrics are those expected at these venues, and the paper situates itself within related work while noting clear limitations (e.g., no sparse-speedup implementation, no iterative prune-and-retrain), which are appropriate to discuss rather than excluding it from scope.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important practical problem of compressing very large NMT models and demonstrates substantial, concrete gains (40% parameter reduction with little loss; 80% pruning with retraining that recovers or exceeds original performance on WMT'14 En–De). Showing magnitude-based pruning works for deep recurrent LSTM attention-based NMT (claimed first application) and that class-blind pruning outperforms other schemes makes the results likely to influence both practitioners seeking smaller models and researchers studying compression. The lack of iterative prune–retrain, exploitation of sparsity for speedups, and comparisons to other saliency methods limits immediate deployment impact but clearly opens useful follow-on research directions. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—compressing large LSTM-based NMT models—and shows compelling empirical gains (40% pruning with little loss and recovery/surpass of baseline after retraining for 80% pruning) on a standard WMT'14 En-De benchmark. Demonstrating that magnitude-based, class-blind pruning works well for recurrent attention-based NMT and being the first to extend pruning to LSTM NMT models is likely to influence practitioners looking to deploy large translation models and to spur follow-up work on sparse training, iterative prune-and-retrain pipelines, and runtime speedups. Limitations (no sparse-runtime exploitation and no comparisons to OBD/OBS) prevent labeling this as transformative, but the results are strong and broadly relevant to the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important practical problem of compressing very large NMT models and demonstrates substantial, concrete gains (40% parameter reduction with little loss; 80% pruning with retraining that recovers or exceeds original performance on WMT'14 En–De). Showing magnitude-based pruning works for deep recurrent LSTM attention-based NMT (claimed first application) and that class-blind pruning outperforms other schemes makes the results likely to influence both practitioners seeking smaller models and researchers studying compression. The lack of iterative prune–retrain, exploitation of sparsity for speedups, and comparisons to other saliency methods limits immediate deployment impact but clearly opens useful follow-on research directions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—compressing large LSTM-based NMT models—and shows compelling empirical gains (40% pruning with little loss and recovery/surpass of baseline after retraining for 80% pruning) on a standard WMT'14 En-De benchmark. Demonstrating that magnitude-based, class-blind pruning works well for recurrent attention-based NMT and being the first to extend pruning to LSTM NMT models is likely to influence practitioners looking to deploy large translation models and to spur follow-up work on sparse training, iterative prune-and-retrain pipelines, and runtime speedups. Limitations (no sparse-runtime exploitation and no comparisons to OBD/OBS) prevent labeling this as transformative, but the results are strong and broadly relevant to the field.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its pruning approach to the unpruned Luong et al. NMT model and to three pruning schemes (class-blind, class-uniform, class-distribution) using the same WMT'14 En–De data and BLEU/perplexity metrics, which is a fair and appropriate baseline setup. However, the authors explicitly note they did not implement iterative prune-and-retrain (as in Han et al.) and did not perform direct comparisons to other established saliency-based pruning methods (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon), which are meaningful omissions. Overall the comparisons are good and fair but missing a few important competing baselines. | B(4): The paper compares the proposed magnitude-based pruning to an unpruned Luong et al. NMT baseline and to three internal pruning schemes, evaluated on the same data and metrics (WMT'14 En–De, newstest2014, perplexity and BLEU), so comparisons are fair. However, it does not include empirical comparisons to other established pruning/saliency methods (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon) or the iterative prune-and-retrain pipeline of Han et al. (2015b), which the authors acknowledge as omitted and future work. Given the thorough related-work discussion and clear analysis of the pruning schemes, the comparisons are good but missing these important external baselines.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its pruning approach to the unpruned Luong et al. NMT model and to three pruning schemes (class-blind, class-uniform, class-distribution) using the same WMT'14 En–De data and BLEU/perplexity metrics, which is a fair and appropriate baseline setup. However, the authors explicitly note they did not implement iterative prune-and-retrain (as in Han et al.) and did not perform direct comparisons to other established saliency-based pruning methods (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon), which are meaningful omissions. Overall the comparisons are good and fair but missing a few important competing baselines.

However, it does not include empirical comparisons to other established pruning/saliency methods (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon) or the iterative prune-and-retrain pipeline of Han et al. (2015b), which the authors acknowledge as omitted and future work. REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper reports hyperparameters and SGD retraining settings and uses public datasets (WMT'14 English–German, newstest2014), and evaluation metrics (perplexity, BLEU) are standard, so much of the experimental setup is specified. However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary, code is not mentioned/available, and lower-level implementation details (exact model wiring beyond referencing the Luong et al. baseline, sparse-matrix handling, random seeds, and any training schedules) are not provided, requiring minor assumptions to fully reimplement. | B(4): The paper reports hyperparameters and training (SGD) settings and specifies the model architecture and pruning schemes, and it uses publicly available datasets (WMT'14 English–German, newstest2014) with standard metrics (perplexity, BLEU). However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary and code is not mentioned, and implementation details for reproducing sparse-matrix masking/runtime behavior are omitted. Given these minor but important missing pieces, the work is mostly reproducible but would require authors to provide preprocessing/code or clarifying implementation details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and SGD retraining settings and uses public datasets (WMT'14 English–German, newstest2014), and evaluation metrics (perplexity, BLEU) are standard, so much of the experimental setup is specified. However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary, code is not mentioned/available, and lower-level implementation details (exact model wiring beyond referencing the Luong et al. baseline, sparse-matrix handling, random seeds, and any training schedules) are not provided, requiring minor assumptions to fully reimplement.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and training (SGD) settings and specifies the model architecture and pruning schemes, and it uses publicly available datasets (WMT'14 English–German, newstest2014) with standard metrics (perplexity, BLEU). However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not described in the summary and code is not mentioned, and implementation details for reproducing sparse-matrix masking/runtime behavior are omitted. Given these minor but important missing pieces, the work is mostly reproducible but would require authors to provide preprocessing/code or clarifying implementation details.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are substantial: they evaluate magnitude-based pruning on a large multi-layer LSTM attention NMT model on WMT'14 En–De, compare three pruning schemes (class-blind/uniform/distribution), report perplexity/BLEU, and show retraining can recover performance even at 80% pruning — which supports the core claims. However, the study omits an iterative prune-and-retrain pipeline (acknowledged), lacks empirical comparisons to other saliency methods (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon), and does not exploit sparsity for speedups, leaving some practical aspects unexplored. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and hyperparameters are given, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and some implementation details for sparse training/runtime are missing. | B(4): The paper runs focused, relevant experiments on WMT’14 En–De (newstest2014), compares three pruning schemes (class-blind, class-uniform, class-distribution), reports BLEU/perplexity at multiple pruning levels (including 40% and 80%) and shows effects of retraining—providing solid ablation and sensitivity on pruning strategy and sparsity levels. However, important gaps remain: no comparison to other saliency methods (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon), no iterative prune-and-retrain pipeline evaluation, limited consideration of runtime/speed benefits from sparsity, and code was not mentioned, which hinders full reproducibility. Related work is reported thorough and limitations are discussed, so overall the experimental depth is strong but missing some key comparative and practical analyses.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are substantial: they evaluate magnitude-based pruning on a large multi-layer LSTM attention NMT model on WMT'14 En–De, compare three pruning schemes (class-blind/uniform/distribution), report perplexity/BLEU, and show retraining can recover performance even at 80% pruning — which supports the core claims. However, the study omits an iterative prune-and-retrain pipeline (acknowledged), lacks empirical comparisons to other saliency methods (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon), and does not exploit sparsity for speedups, leaving some practical aspects unexplored. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and hyperparameters are given, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and some implementation details for sparse training/runtime are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs focused, relevant experiments on WMT’14 En–De (newstest2014), compares three pruning schemes (class-blind, class-uniform, class-distribution), reports BLEU/perplexity at multiple pruning levels (including 40% and 80%) and shows effects of retraining—providing solid ablation and sensitivity on pruning strategy and sparsity levels. However, important gaps remain: no comparison to other saliency methods (e.g., Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon), no iterative prune-and-retrain pipeline evaluation, limited consideration of runtime/speed benefits from sparsity, and code was not mentioned, which hinders full reproducibility. Related work is reported thorough and limitations are discussed, so overall the experimental depth is strong but missing some key comparative and practical analyses.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from problem statement to method to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (magnitude-based pruning for LSTM NMT, pruning results, and comparison of pruning schemes) are stated early. Notation and methodology are presented with sufficient detail (hyperparameters reported, experimental setup on WMT'14 EN-DE), and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective, and the writing quality is adequate, suggesting only minor presentation issues rather than major clarity problems. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear logical flow from claims to method to experiments and conclusion (summary lists methodology, datasets, baselines, metrics, and stated limitations). The main contributions are stated early and explicitly (magnitude-based pruning results, pruning schemes comparison, extension to LSTM NMT). Notation and experimental details seem clear enough for reproduction (hyperparameters are reported), which supports consistent presentation. Writing quality is described as adequate, though a few presentation details (e.g., code availability, and explicit mention of figures/tables in the summary) are not highlighted, suggesting only minor presentation issues.

Notation and methodology are presented with sufficient detail (hyperparameters reported, experimental setup on WMT'14 EN-DE), and related work coverage is thorough. Notation and experimental details seem clear enough for reproduction (hyperparameters are reported), which supports consistent presentation. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are supported by experiments on WMT'14 En–De showing magnitude-based pruning achieves ~40% compression with little performance loss and that retraining can recover (and reportedly surpass) performance even at 80% pruning; they also empirically find class-blind pruning outperforms the other two schemes and report results on an LSTM-based attention NMT. The methodology appears generally sound: an appropriate unpruned baseline is used, alternative pruning schemes are compared, and BLEU/perplexity and hyperparameters are reported. However, the paper omits iterative prune-and-retrain used in related work, lacks comparisons to other saliency methods (e.g., OBD/OBS), does not exploit sparsity for speedups, and does not report statistical significance — limitations that do not invalidate the core experimental findings but reduce completeness of the evaluation. | B(4): The paper’s main empirical claims (e.g., 40% pruning with little loss and recovery/superiority at 80% with retraining, class-blind pruning outperforming other schemes, and extension to LSTM-based NMT) are supported by experiments on WMT'14 En–De using perplexity and BLEU against a sensible unpruned attention-based baseline. The methodology (magnitude-based pruning, three comparison schemes, retraining) and reported hyperparameters appear sound. Minor technical limitations—no iterative prune-and-retrain pipeline (as in Han et al.), no comparison to other saliency methods like Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon, and no report of statistical significance—limit the completeness of the evaluation but do not invalidate the core experimental findings.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are supported by experiments on WMT'14 En–De showing magnitude-based pruning achieves ~40% compression with little performance loss and that retraining can recover (and reportedly surpass) performance even at 80% pruning; they also empirically find class-blind pruning outperforms the other two schemes and report results on an LSTM-based attention NMT. The methodology appears generally sound: an appropriate unpruned baseline is used, alternative pruning schemes are compared, and BLEU/perplexity and hyperparameters are reported. However, the paper omits iterative prune-and-retrain used in related work, lacks comparisons to other saliency methods (e.g., OBD/OBS), does not exploit sparsity for speedups, and does not report statistical significance — limitations that do not invalidate the core experimental findings but reduce completeness of the evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main empirical claims (e.g., 40% pruning with little loss and recovery/superiority at 80% with retraining, class-blind pruning outperforming other schemes, and extension to LSTM-based NMT) are supported by experiments on WMT'14 En–De using perplexity and BLEU against a sensible unpruned attention-based baseline. The methodology (magnitude-based pruning, three comparison schemes, retraining) and reported hyperparameters appear sound. Minor technical limitations—no iterative prune-and-retrain pipeline (as in Han et al.), no comparison to other saliency methods like Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon, and no report of statistical significance—limit the completeness of the evaluation but do not invalidate the core experimental findings.

main_claims: Magnitude-based weight pruning effectively compresses NMT models, reducing parameter count with limited performance loss.

An NMT model with over 200 million parameters can be pruned by 40% with very little performance loss on the WMT'14 English–German task, and with retraining an 80%-pruned model can recover and even surpass the original performance.
Class-blind pruning outperforms class-uniform and class-distribution pruning schemes for this NMT architecture.
The paper extends the magnitude-based pruning approach to recurrent LSTM-based NMT models and claims to be the first to do so.
_summary.methodology: Apply magnitude-based pruning (remove weights with smallest absolute value) under three schemes (class-blind, class-uniform, class-distribution), keep binary mask matrices for pruned locations, and retrain the sparse networks with specified SGD settings on a deep multi-layer LSTM attention-based NMT model.
baselines: Unpruned attention-based NMT model from Luong et al. (2015a)
limitations: Did not implement iterative prune-and-retrain pipeline used in Han et al. (2015b), which they state would likely yield further improvements.
They did not exploit sparsity for speedups in training or runtime (sparse representations/multiplications not utilized).
They did not perform a comparative study against other pruning/saliency methods like Optimal Brain Damage/Optimal Brain Surgeon and note this as future work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Neural Machine Translation (model compression via pruning)
